claims.claim_text: The Knight Capital August 2012 deployment error involved one server being missed, caused 45 minutes of erroneous trading, and led to losses of more than $460 million.
claims.rationale: The incident is real and precisely citable, but the strongest primary source is on sec.gov, which is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Cloud Build is a managed build service that runs each build in a fresh VM and executes build steps in containers.
claims.rationale: The Cloud Build overview directly describes per-build VMs and containerized build steps.

claims.claim_text: Cloud Build provides `/workspace` as a default shared volume so files written there persist across steps in the same build.
claims.rationale: The pass-data-between-steps guide explicitly documents `/workspace` as the default volume available to subsequent steps.

claims.claim_text: Using the `images` field both pushes images after a successful build and generates proper build provenance metadata, framed here as an SBOM.
claims.anchor_text: While we explicitly run a `docker push` in Step 2, defining the image in the `images` array instructs Cloud Build to automatically push the image upon successful completion of the build and, crucially, to generate proper build provenance metadata (Software Bill of Materials) which is vital for software supply chain security.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Defining the image in the `images` array tells Cloud Build to push the built image after a successful build, and Cloud Build can generate build provenance for supported images stored in Artifact Registry.
claims.rationale: Cloud Build docs support automatic image pushing and provenance generation, but not the parenthetical equivalence to an SBOM.

claims.claim_text: Cloud Build provides built-in substitutions such as `$PROJECT_ID`, `$BUILD_ID`, `$COMMIT_SHA`, `$SHORT_SHA`, `$BRANCH_NAME`, `$TAG_NAME`, `$REPO_NAME`, and `$REVISION_ID`.
claims.rationale: The substitutions guide lists these built-in variables and distinguishes general versus trigger-provided substitutions.

claims.claim_text: User-defined Cloud Build substitutions must start with an underscore and can be overridden with `--substitutions`.
claims.rationale: The substitutions guide explicitly documents underscore-prefixed user variables and CLI overrides.

claims.claim_text: Google-provided builders are cached directly on worker nodes, so pulling them has virtually zero network latency.
claims.anchor_text: Because these images are cached directly on the Cloud Build worker nodes, pulling them incurs virtually zero network latency, ensuring your pipeline starts executing your code almost instantly.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Google provides maintained builder images for common toolchains, and Cloud Build can also run public container images as build steps.
claims.rationale: The docs support maintained builder images and Docker Hub caching behavior, but not this direct worker-node caching and latency guarantee.

claims.claim_text: Cloud Build can use any publicly available container image as a builder step.
claims.span_hint: section: Using Any Docker Image as a Builder
claims.rationale: The builders documentation explicitly says Cloud Build enables use of any publicly available image to execute tasks.

claims.claim_text: Cloud Build runs steps sequentially by default and supports DAG-style parallel execution with `id` and `waitFor`, including `waitFor: ['-']` for immediate start.
claims.rationale: The step-order guide directly documents sequential defaults, dependency graphs, and the special `-` start dependency.

claims.claim_text: Cloud Build GitHub triggers support branch, pull request, and tag patterns, plus comment-control options for pull requests.
claims.rationale: The GitHub trigger docs describe trigger events and the available comment-control behaviors.

claims.claim_text: Cloud Build GitLab connections are regional and use access tokens that are stored in Secret Manager.
claims.rationale: The GitLab host-connection guide explicitly requires a region and states the tokens are stored in Secret Manager.

claims.claim_text: When run locally, `gcloud builds submit` packages the local directory and uploads it as build input; builds can also be submitted from a Cloud Storage archive.
claims.rationale: The manual-build guide describes local source submission behavior and Cloud Storage archive inputs.

claims.claim_text: Cloud Build recommends user-specified service accounts to follow least privilege because default service accounts may have broader access than needed.
claims.rationale: Google’s guidance explicitly recommends user-specified service accounts for least privilege and warns default accounts may be broader than necessary.

claims.claim_text: The legacy default Cloud Build service account historically had sweeping administrative privileges across the entire project.
claims.anchor_text: By default, Cloud Build historically leveraged a single, powerful "legacy" service account (`[PROJECT_NUMBER]@cloudbuild.gserviceaccount.com`) which possessed sweeping administrative privileges across the entire project.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Historically, relying on the default Cloud Build service account could give a build more access than it needed, which is why production pipelines should use narrowly scoped user-specified service accounts.
claims.rationale: Current docs describe default accounts as potentially broader than necessary, but not as a blanket project-wide admin role.

claims.claim_text: Cloud Deploy pipeline and target definitions must be supplied as separate YAML files rather than a single multi-document YAML stream.
claims.span_hint: section: Setting Up a Delivery Pipeline
claims.anchor_text: To resolve parsing complexities, it is critical to supply these as independent, well-formed YAML files rather than concatenating them in a single stream.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Cloud Deploy lets you define the delivery pipeline and targets either in one YAML file or in separate files, depending on which layout fits your workflow better.
claims.rationale: Cloud Deploy docs explicitly state that pipeline and target definitions can be in the same file or in separate files.

claims.claim_text: Cloud Deploy is a managed continuous delivery service built around delivery pipelines, targets, releases, and rollouts.
claims.span_hint: section: Cloud Deploy: Continuous Delivery Pipelines
claims.rationale: The overview page directly describes Cloud Deploy’s delivery-pipeline, release, target, and rollout model.

claims.claim_text: Cloud Deploy supports deployments to GKE Enterprise user clusters in addition to GKE and Cloud Run.
claims.span_hint: section: Cloud Deploy: Continuous Delivery Pipelines
claims.rationale: The GKE Enterprise target guide directly documents Cloud Deploy support for those targets.

claims.claim_text: Cloud Deploy supports target-level manual approval as well as canary strategies with Cloud Run automatic traffic control and deployment verification.
claims.span_hint: section: Setting Up a Delivery Pipeline
claims.rationale: The target-configuration docs cover `requireApproval`, and linked Cloud Deploy docs cover canary and verification support.

claims.claim_text: Cloud Deploy rollback creates a new rollout based on the last successful release for the target unless a different release is specified.
claims.span_hint: section: Setting Up a Delivery Pipeline
claims.rationale: The rollback guide explicitly describes rollback behavior and the default to the last successful release.

claims.claim_text: Cloud Build substitutions are thoroughly logged and visible in plain text throughout build history and the console, so they should never be used for secrets.
claims.anchor_text: Cloud Build substitutions are thoroughly logged and visible in plain text throughout the build history and GCP console interface.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Do not pass secrets through build substitutions; use Secret Manager with `availableSecrets` and `secretEnv` so secrets are provided only to the steps that need them.
claims.rationale: The supported security guidance is to use Secret Manager, while the module’s blanket claim about substitution visibility is not directly documented in that exact form.

claims.claim_text: Cloud Build’s default pool has no minimum fees, gives the first 120 build-minutes per day free on `e2-medium`, and therefore costs literally nothing for a team doing 10 five-minute builds per day.
claims.anchor_text: **Cloud Build's default worker pool runs on Google-managed infrastructure** with no minimum fees. You only pay for the build minutes consumed. The first 120 build-minutes per day are free for the `e2-medium` machine type. For a team doing 10 builds per day averaging 5 minutes each, the CI/CD platform costs literally nothing.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Cloud Build's default pool is Google-managed and billed by build-minute, with a monthly free tier on the default pool that can make small-team CI/CD very inexpensive.
claims.rationale: The module’s free-tier numbers and cost conclusion are outdated against current pricing.

claims.claim_text: The shared `/workspace` is a mounted directory rather than a Docker volume and therefore performs at native filesystem speed.
claims.anchor_text: The workspace is a mounted directory, not a Docker volume, so it performs at native filesystem speed.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The `/workspace` directory is shared across build steps, so later steps can reuse files produced earlier in the same build.
claims.rationale: The docs support `/workspace` persistence, but not this implementation-specific performance characterization.

claims.claim_text: Private worker pools run inside your VPC and support custom machine types up to 32 vCPUs.
claims.anchor_text: **Private worker pools run inside your VPC**, allowing builds to access private resources (private Artifact Registry, internal APIs, databases) without exposing them to the internet. They also support custom machine types up to 32 vCPUs for faster builds. Private pools are essential for enterprises with strict network security requirements.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Private worker pools can be connected to your VPC so builds can reach private resources, and they offer more machine-type choices and network controls than the default pool.
claims.rationale: Official docs describe private pools as Google-managed workers that can peer into your VPC, not as workers literally running inside it.

claims.claim_text: Using kaniko layer caching in Artifact Registry can reduce build times by 50-80% for large Docker images.
claims.anchor_text: **Cloud Build supports build caching through `kaniko`**, a tool that builds Docker images without a Docker daemon. Kaniko can cache intermediate layers in Artifact Registry, so subsequent builds that share base layers skip the redundant steps. This can reduce build times by 50-80% for large Docker images with many dependencies.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Cloud Build can use image-layer caching to speed up rebuilds, especially when later Dockerfile layers change more often than earlier ones.
claims.rationale: The performance lesson is sound, but the exact `50-80%` claim is an illustrative benchmark without a primary-source figure.

claims.claim_text: Cloud Build’s default timeout is 10 minutes.
claims.anchor_text: Default 10-minute timeout is too short for large builds
claims.suggested_rewrite: The default build timeout may be shorter than larger pipelines need, so set `timeout` explicitly for long-running builds.
claims.rationale: The current build-config schema documents a 60-minute default build timeout, so the specific `10-minute` claim is incorrect.

further_reading.title: Cloud Build Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers the core execution model, build steps, pools, and security concepts behind Cloud Build.

further_reading.title: Cloud Deploy Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains delivery pipelines, targets, releases, promotions, approvals, and rollouts.

further_reading.title: Use Secrets from Secret Manager
further_reading.why_relevant: Shows the supported pattern for handling build-time secrets safely in Cloud Build.

notes: Quiz prompts, quiz answers, and hands-on exercise steps were intentionally excluded per pipeline rules; claims were limited to inline prose and table cells that carry factual vendor, incident, security, pricing, or benchmark assertions.
